user.name: Jeffrey Towson 陶迅
user.description: I help increase online growth and strengthen digital AI moats. Digital & AI mgmt consultant. Get my free email and Tech Strategy podcast.

fullText:
HOW HUAWEI’S TAU (τ) SCALING LAW WILL CHANGE SEMICONDUCTOR COMPETITION

Huawei’s new approach for designing quasi-3D circuitry is big news. 

Everyone is talking about the: 

1-Technical aspects, which are formidable. Lots of talk about LogicFolding, compute density and heat dissipation. 

2-Political implications. What this means for Chinese and US tech supply chains.

What people are missing is:

Huawei’s new architecture will change the dynamics of semiconductor competition.

-------
GPU industry structure has long been shaped by:

-Nvidia’s global dominance, based on R&D scale and standardization network effects (hello CUDA)

-Really big and highly specialized capital expenditures by TSMC, ASML, 

Samsung and others. You need to spend tens of billions of dollars annually on cutting edge fabs. 

This second point is also why the purchases of +$200 million, super specialized EUV lithography machines were so important. And that is why the US sanctioned that particular chokepoint.

Huawei’s new architecture is a strategy for bypassing these chokepoints (somewhat). 

Here's the take-away:

-Huawei’s new architecture will shift semiconductors from mostly a game of Big, Specialized Equipment Buying to

-A game that is also focused on Multi-Level Co-Design, Physical Architecture Innovation and Algorithmic Routing

What does that mean?

Both advanced EUV geometric shrinking and 3D architecture can be used to compound performance gains.

And competition will shift more towards having the best optimization for this very complex architecture. 

That means:

1-Multi-Level Co-Design

2-Architectural Innovation

3-Optimization of Algorithmic Routing

Basically, watch for engineers spending a lot more time on the increasingly complex architecture, advanced geometric mathematics and routing algorithms. In addition to the standard focus on materials science. 

And I don’t think the big focus on materials science is going anywhere. Especially with heat dissipation being a major challenge in 3D circuitry. 

I also don’t think Moore’s Law and big fabs are going anywhere. 

But I do think competitive performance will be more and more dependent on achieving Tau Scaling with physical architecture and routing algorithms over time.

It’s going to be fascinating.
 
https://t.co/jJMrTeb2G0

user.name: DeepLearning.AI
user.description: We are an education technology company with the mission to grow and connect the global AI community.

fullText:
AI agents seem to be increasingly capable of performing economically valuable tasks, but current benchmarks measure this capability only narrowly.

Zora Z. Wang and colleagues at Carnegie Mellon University and Stanford University mapped examples drawn from agent benchmarks to statistics that represent U.S. labor. The mapping revealed a mismatch between the tests, which generally emphasize software development, and the more varied work most people do.

Read the full article in The Batch: https://t.co/RVm3dhlRIE

user.name: Naveen Rao
user.description: CEO @unconvai. Former CEO MosaicML/Databricks & Nervana/IntelAI. Neuro + CS. I like to build stuff that will eventually learn how to build other stuff.

fullText:
I know it's en vogue to crap on SaaS right now, but there is/was an important function there: cross-customer pattern sharing. SaaS observed how their segment used a tool, then built that repeated pattern into the tool without leaking customer proprietary info. They get paid if they do it well.
In AI, I DO NOT want proprietary info going to the model provider. But, as a user, I do benefit if some aspects of what I do were built into the AI model. There needs to be a clean way to manage this and monetize it. And it's more than just prompt management.
